question.text: Which of the following statements about Muslim succession law is INCORRECT?

answer.text: Wasiyat (will) is limited to 1/3 of net assets
Sunni law follows per capita distribution while Shia law follows per stirpes distribution
Under Muslim law, ancestral property is recognized as a separate category with different succession rules
A Muslim will can be oral, written, or even made by gestures

explanation.text: **The Logic:** There is NO concept of ancestral property in Muslim law — all property is treated equally regardless of how it was acquired. This is a key distinction from Hindu law where ancestral property has special rules. **The Trap:** Candidates familiar with Hindu law often assume ancestral property concepts apply universally. Under Muslim law, Wasiyat is limited to 1/3 of net assets, and the will can be oral, written, or by gestures. A minor aged 15+ can also make a will. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM X-B tests differences between Hindu and Muslim succession. Also note: Escheat applies when no legal heir exists — government inherits the property. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When advising Muslim clients, remember that the 1/3 Wasiyat limit means 2/3 of the estate MUST go per succession rules. This limits estate planning flexibility compared to Hindu law.
